capacity_source: Year-end cumulative grid-connected solar PV capacity for Spain, compiled from Red Electrica de Espana (REE) data as tabulated in the Wikipedia 'Solar power in Spain' article (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_power_in_Spain), with 2023 (25,549 MW) and 2024 (32,043 MW) figures from REE press reporting (https://www.ree.es/en/press-office/press-release/news/press-release/2025/02/solar-pv-takes-lead-spains-installed-power-capacity). The 2024 value is REE's figure as of 31 Jan 2025, used here as year-end 2024. The 2025 figure (41,500 MW, a 26.6% increase over 2024) is from REE's renewable energies report (https://www.sistemaelectrico-ree.es/en/renewable-energies-report/sun/installed-capacity/photovoltaic-solar-sunpower) and confirmed by the REE 'Spanish Electricity System in 2025' press release (https://www.ree.es/en/press-office/news/press-release/2026/03/the-spanish-electricity-system-in-2025-electricity-demand-generation-and-installed-power-capacity-increase).
capacity_note: Values are year-end CUMULATIVE installed solar photovoltaic capacity in MW. They are PV-only and EXCLUDE concentrated solar power (CSP/solar thermal). The series reflects REE grid-connected capacity in the Spanish peninsular/national electricity system, so it counts grid-connected installations and may under-count distributed self-consumption capacity (industry body UNEF reports higher totals, e.g. ~32.5 GW for 2023 and ~40 GW for 2024, because it includes self-consumption; when self-consumption is included Spain's total solar capacity reached ~49.5 GW by end-2025 and crossed 50 GW in early 2026). The series clearly shows the 2008 boom (637 MW at end-2007 to 3,355 MW at end-2008 under RD 661/2007), the 2009-2018 near-freeze, the recovery beginning 2019 after the repeal of the 'sun tax' and liberalization of self-consumption, and the acceleration from 2020 onward driven by the REER auction framework and NextGenerationEU funds. Caveat: no clean year-end PV-only figures were found for 2004-2005, so the series begins at 2006; the exact 2007/2008 cutoff can vary slightly by source (some report ~690 MW end-2007 / ~3,463 MW end-2008).

events.title: Feed-in tariff scheme begins
events.label: RD 661/2007
events.detail: Royal Decree 661/2007 (25 May 2007, BOE 26 May) set generous feed-in tariffs for renewables under the 'special regime', triggering Spain's solar PV investment boom in 2007-2008.

events.title: New PV tariffs cut ~30%
events.label: RD 1578/2008
events.detail: Royal Decree 1578/2008 (26 Sept 2008) cut feed-in tariffs roughly 30% for new PV plants registered after 29 Sept 2008 and capped annual new capacity, beginning the containment of the solar sector.

events.title: PV tariffs cut up to 45%, lifetime capped
events.label: RD 1565/2010
events.detail: Royal Decree 1565/2010 (19 Nov 2010) cut PV feed-in tariffs for installations under RD 1578/2008 (up to 45% for ground-mounted) and limited the tariff-eligible lifetime to 25 years.

events.title: Output caps imposed on existing PV plants
events.label: RDL 14/2010
events.detail: Royal Decree-Law 14/2010 (23 Dec 2010) introduced 'equivalent operating hours' caps on subsidised PV output and other measures to correct the tariff deficit, reducing remuneration for existing plants.

events.title: Moratorium on new renewable registrations
events.label: RDL 1/2012
events.detail: Royal Decree-Law 1/2012 (27 Jan 2012) imposed a moratorium suspending the pre-allocation registry and removing economic incentives for new renewable installations, effectively halting new PV registrations.

events.title: 7% electricity generation tax introduced
events.label: Law 15/2012
events.detail: Law 15/2012 (27 Dec 2012) on fiscal measures for energy sustainability created a 7% tax on the value of all electricity generation (in force 1 Jan 2013), cutting net returns for renewable producers.

events.title: Feed-in tariff regime abolished entirely
events.label: RDL 9/2013
events.detail: Royal Decree-Law 9/2013 (12 July 2013, BOE 13 July) abolished the feed-in tariff/'special regime' remuneration, replacing it for all plants with a new 'reasonable return' specific remuneration scheme.

events.title: New Electricity Sector Law entrenches reform
events.label: Law 24/2013
events.detail: Law 24/2013 (26 Dec 2013), the new Electricity Sector Law, consolidated the reform, entrenching the 'reasonable return' model and ending the prior special-regime support framework for renewables.

events.title: Retroactive 'reasonable return' regime applied
events.label: RD 413/2014
events.detail: Royal Decree 413/2014 (6 June 2014) implemented the new 'specific remuneration' regime based on a 'reasonable rate of return', applied retroactively to existing plants and triggering ECT arbitration claims.

events.title: 'Sun tax' on solar self-consumption imposed
events.label: RD 900/2015
events.detail: Royal Decree 900/2015 (9 Oct 2015) imposed charges on electricity generated and self-consumed by solar panel owners — dubbed the 'impuesto al sol' (sun tax). Described as one of the world's most restrictive self-consumption regulations, it effectively halted distributed solar growth for three years.

events.title: Utility-scale solar auctions: ~3.9 GW awarded with Dec 2019 deadline
events.label: 2017 Renewable Auctions
events.detail: Spain held two competitive renewable energy auctions in 2017 (May 17 and July), awarding ~3,909 MW to solar PV — concentrated in the May auction — plus ~4,107 MW to wind. Winning projects faced a hard deadline of December 31, 2019 to connect to the grid or forfeit bonds. Developers spent 2017–2018 securing permits, land, and financing; over 3,700 MW of that solar capacity then connected in a single surge in 2019, driving the spike visible in the data. This — not the residential self-consumption rules — was the primary cause of the 2019 boom.

events.title: Intra-EU BIT arbitration declared incompatible with EU law
events.label: Achmea (CJEU, C-284/16)
events.detail: In Case C-284/16 (Achmea, 6 March 2018) the CJEU held that arbitration clauses in intra-EU bilateral investment treaties are incompatible with EU law, undermining intra-EU investor-state arbitration.

events.title: First large subsidy-free solar plant: merchant era begins
events.label: Don Rodrigo PPA (BayWa / Statkraft)
events.detail: On 10 April 2018, BayWa r.e. and Statkraft signed a 15-year PPA for Don Rodrigo, a 170 MW solar plant near Seville — one of the first large-scale subsidy-free solar projects in Europe. The deal proved that Spanish solar could attract private financing purely on merchant terms, without any government subsidy, and opened a wave of corporate PPA-backed development running in parallel with the 2017 auction pipeline.

events.title: Sun tax repealed
events.label: RDL 15/2018
events.detail: Royal Decree-Law 15/2018 (5 Oct 2018) abolished the sun tax on self-consumed electricity, simplified administrative procedures for grid connection, and allowed collective self-consumption by building communities — reversing Spain's most punitive solar restriction.

events.title: Self-consumption fully liberalized
events.label: RD 244/2019
events.detail: Royal Decree 244/2019 (5 April 2019) established the complete administrative, technical and economic framework for solar self-consumption, introducing net billing, removing remaining barriers, and allowing communities of neighbours to share solar installations. Triggered Spain's distributed solar boom.

events.title: New renewable energy auction regime launched
events.label: RD 960/2020
events.detail: Royal Decree 960/2020 (3 Nov 2020) established the Economic Regime for Renewable Energy (REER), replacing the abolished feed-in tariff with technology-neutral competitive price auctions. The first auction (26 Jan 2021) awarded 3 GW (2 GW solar + 1 GW wind) and sparked Spain's post-2020 utility-scale renewables surge.

events.title: NextGenerationEU solar grants deployed
events.label: RD 477/2021
events.detail: Royal Decree 477/2021 (June 2021) deployed €1.3 billion in EU Recovery Funds (NextGenerationEU) via Spain's Plan de Recuperación, Transformación y Resiliencia, offering grants to homes and businesses for solar panels, batteries and heat pumps. The programme was later expanded by €505M (June 2022), accelerating distributed solar adoption.

events.title: Intra-EU ECT arbitration declared incompatible with EU law
events.label: Komstroy (CJEU, C-741/19)
events.detail: In Case C-741/19 (Moldova v Komstroy, 2 Sept 2021) the CJEU extended Achmea to the Energy Charter Treaty, ruling that intra-EU ECT arbitration is incompatible with EU law.

events.title: Spain announces withdrawal from the ECT
events.label: Spain ECT withdrawal notice
events.detail: Spain confirmed its intention to withdraw from the Energy Charter Treaty (announced April 2024; formal notice to the depositary given 27 June 2024), part of a coordinated EU exit. (Exact announcement date may vary slightly by source.)

events.title: EC rules paying ECT awards to EU investors is illegal state aid
events.label: Commission Decision (EU) 2025/1235 — Antin
events.detail: Commission Decision (EU) 2025/1235 (24 March 2025) found the ICSID arbitration award ordering Spain to pay Antin (~EUR 101m) to be illegal and incompatible State aid, barring its payment or enforcement.

events.title: Spain's ECT exit takes effect (20-yr sunset applies)
events.label: Spain leaves ECT
events.detail: Spain's withdrawal from the Energy Charter Treaty took effect on 28 June 2025, one year after its notice to the depositary, though the ECT's 20-year sunset clause keeps existing investments protected.

pv_investment_note: Annual solar PV private capital expenditure, estimated from year-over-year grid-connected capacity additions (REE data) multiplied by IRENA utility-scale PV system cost benchmarks (EUR/kW) per year. Cost curve adapted from IRENA Renewable Power Generation Costs series and BNEF solar capex tracking. Excludes subsidies/grants. The 2008 spike (~€13.6B) reflects Spain's FiT-era installation boom when ~2.7 GW was added at pre-cost-reduction prices (~€5,000/kW). The 2014–2018 additions were tiny (<20 MW/yr), so investment is negligible. The 2019–2025 recovery shows large investment at dramatically lower unit costs.
pv_subsidy_note: Annual solar PV government support cost — feed-in tariff premiums 2006–2013, then 'specific remuneration' regime (RD 413/2014) from 2014 onward. Confirmed data points (filled dots on chart): 2007 = €215M and 2013 = €3,267M from Mir-Artigues, Cerdá & del Río (2015) 'Analyzing the impact of cost-containment mechanisms on the profitability of solar PV plants in Spain', Renewable & Sustainable Energy Reviews 46; 2009 = €2.6B from IISD and multiple sources. All other years are estimates. Negative 2022 value reflects the Iberian exception mechanism under which Spain recovered excess revenues from renewable producers when spot prices far exceeded reference prices.
